content.text: Cowork is designed for team operation, not individual use. A single goal created by one person is useful; a shared library of goals maintained by a team, with common connectors, shared projects, and workspace-level policies, is where the platform's real productivity multiplier emerges.
that governs who can do what, how to deploy Cowork across a team or enterprise, and the administrative controls that make large-scale operation safe and auditable.

attrs.alt: Cowork workspace hierarchy with roles diagram

content.text: The diagram shows two parallel workspaces (Engineering and Sales Ops) under a single organization. Each workspace has its own shared connectors and projects. Roles — Admin, Member, Viewer, Guest — are scoped per workspace.

A Cowork workspace is the primary unit of team collaboration. It groups together a set of users, a set of connectors, and a set of projects. Within a workspace, any member can use any workspace connector; any project is visible to all workspace members (with role-appropriate access); and workspace-level policies apply uniformly to all goals.
The four-level hierarchy controls both access and isolation:
All workspaces, billing, SSO configuration, org-wide audit log
Users, shared connectors, workspace policy, projects
Workspace admin (typically team lead or department head)
A group of related goals with shared context and memory
Project owner (typically individual contributor or team lead)
A single scheduled or on-demand agentic task

Isolation is enforced at the workspace boundary. A goal in the Engineering workspace cannot use connectors from the Sales Ops workspace, and an admin of one workspace does not have admin rights in another. Cross-workspace sharing is not supported — it is a deliberate isolation boundary, not a missing feature.
A common mistake is creating one workspace for an entire company. Multiple workspaces are preferable when:
the Sales team's CRM data should not be accessible to Engineering goals, and vice versa
a legal team handling privileged documents needs different connector scoping and audit requirements than a marketing team
a regulated finance team needs strict sandbox-first policies; a product team building internal tools may be comfortable with more automation
workspace administration should sit with the person accountable for the goals in that workspace, not a shared IT admin who doesn't understand the goals' content

Roles are assigned per workspace, not globally. The same user can be an Admin in the Engineering workspace and a Member in the Design workspace. Role assignments are managed by workspace admins in the workspace settings panel.
A common team structure for a 10-person team:
team lead + technical lead; responsible for connector authorization, workspace policy, and user management
individual contributors who create and maintain goals for their own work areas
stakeholders who consume goal outputs (summaries, reports) but do not create goals themselves
external contractors or cross-team collaborators who need visibility into one project's outputs
Tip: start all new users as Members, not Admins. Promote to Admin only when they need to authorize a connector or set workspace policy. Admin rights should track accountability, not seniority.
Shared Projects: Team Collaboration on Goals

A project groups related goals with shared context. In a team setting, projects are the primary vehicle for maintaining institutional knowledge about a workflow — the goal history, the connector scope, and the instruction set evolution are all preserved at the project level.
Every project maintains a memory store: documents, notes, and structured data that any goal in the project can read. In a team context, this means:
A sales project's memory can hold customer personas, current pricing, and ICP criteria — every goal in the project reads this context without the creator needing to re-specify it

An engineering project's memory can hold the team's on-call rotation, the system architecture diagram, and the incident classification rubric — goals that draft postmortems or route alerts read these automatically

When team knowledge changes (a new pricing tier, a new system component), updating the project memory propagates the change to all goals in the project without editing each goal's instruction set individually
(visible and runnable by all workspace members) or

(visible only to the creator, but using workspace connectors). A good rule of thumb: goals that produce outputs consumed by the team should be shared; goals that produce personal drafts or notes should be personal. Shared goals in a project form the team's reusable goal library.

When a team member leaves, their goals do not disappear — they remain in the workspace. Workspace admins can transfer ownership of any goal to another member. This is critical for ensuring that scheduled goals (weekly reports, daily summaries) continue running when their original creator departs.
Workspace admins have access to the workspace admin panel, which covers user management, connector management, policy configuration, and audit log access.
Enter email address and select role (Admin / Member / Viewer / Guest)
For Guest: also select which project the guest can access
The invited user receives an email with a join link; if SSO is configured, they authenticate via SSO on first join
For bulk invites (enterprise): upload a CSV of email addresses and roles, or use SCIM provisioning if the IdP supports it
Enterprise workspaces can configure SAML 2.0 SSO, typically via Okta, Azure AD, or Google Workspace. Once configured:
password-based login is disabled for the workspace; users who lose SSO access automatically lose Cowork access
only email addresses under the verified domain can join the workspace, even if someone has the join link
user accounts are created and deprovisioned automatically when accounts are created or deleted in the IdP — no manual off-boarding required

# SAML SSO configuration (workspace admin settings)
sso:
  provider: okta                          # or azure_ad, google_workspace, generic_saml
  entity_id: https://cowork.anthropic.com/saml/acme-engineering
  sso_url: https://acme.okta.com/app/saml/exk.../sso/saml
  certificate: |
    -----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----
    MIIDpDCCAoygAwIBAgI...
    -----END CERTIFICATE-----

domain_verification:
  verified_domains:
    - acme.com
    - acme-corp.com
  enforce: true    # users with unverified domains cannot join

scim:
  enabled: true
  scim_url: https://cowork.anthropic.com/scim/v2/acme-engineering
  token: scim-token-goes-here  # generated in workspace settings
Organizations with multiple workspaces and large user counts get access to enterprise-level controls managed from the org admin panel rather than individual workspace settings.

The org audit log captures every significant event across all workspaces: user joins and role changes, connector authorizations and revocations, goal creations and deletions, approval decisions, scope violation attempts, and safety events. The log is immutable — entries cannot be deleted — and can be exported to a SIEM or compliance system.

# Audit log entry format (structured JSON lines)
{
  "timestamp": "2026-04-15T14:32:01Z",
  "org_id": "org_acme",
  "workspace": "engineering",
  "actor": "sarah@acme.com",
  "actor_role": "admin",
  "event_type": "connector_authorized",
  "details": {
    "connector": "google_drive",
    "scope": "/Engineering-Docs/",
    "authorized_for": "workspace"
  },
  "ip": "203.0.113.42",
  "session_id": "sess_7x9k2m"
}

Enterprise org admins can view usage analytics broken down by workspace, project, and goal: API calls, connector reads and writes, goal run counts, and token consumption. This data supports two governance use cases:
map Cowork costs to the teams or projects consuming the most resources; useful for internal chargeback or budget planning
identify high-frequency goals that might benefit from caching or consolidation, or low-usage shared goals that could be removed from the library

Enterprise accounts are billed at the organization level, not per workspace. Usage is aggregated across all workspaces on a single invoice. Workspace admins can see their workspace's usage share but cannot see other workspaces' data. Org admins see the full breakdown.

Getting a team from zero to productive with Cowork is a staged process. Jumping straight to full automation on day one reliably produces poor outcomes — goals that are too broad, connectors authorized too liberally, and users who don't understand what the goals are doing.
Identify 2–3 high-frequency, low-risk workflows: tasks the team currently does manually on a weekly basis that are well-defined and produce easily-verifiable output
Create a sandbox-only workspace for the first two weeks: all goals run sandbox-first, no auto-approved live runs; team reviews every sandbox report before approving
Authorize connectors with minimum scope: start with read-only for all connectors; only add write scope when a goal has proven its read correctness
Build the goal library in shared projects: as goals prove out, move them from personal to shared so the team accumulates a reusable library
Promote successful patterns to workspace policy: encode what works as workspace defaults (e.g., sandbox_first for all new goals), so new goals start from a safe baseline
Relax scope incrementally: as specific goals prove reliable through sandbox review, selectively enable auto-run for those goal types while keeping sandbox-first as the default for new ones

For teams larger than five people, write a one-page Cowork governance document that covers: what connectors are authorized in each workspace, who the workspace admins are, what goal types require sandbox review, and what the process is for creating a new shared goal. This prevents the workspace from growing into an unauditable collection of goals that nobody understands.
A shared goal without documentation is technical debt. A shared goal with a description, a sandbox test run on record, and a named owner is an asset.
Managing Connector Health at Workspace Scale

Workspace connectors are shared resources. When a connector's OAuth token expires, every goal in the workspace that depends on it fails silently — not because the goals are broken, but because the connector's authorization needs refreshing. Managing connector health is an ongoing admin responsibility, not a one-time setup task.

OAuth tokens for most services (Google, Slack) expire after a period of inactivity or after a fixed time. Cowork refreshes tokens automatically as long as the connector is actively used. Tokens that go unused for more than 90 days may be revoked by the provider and require re-authorization.
Warning badge in workspace connector list
Goals with this connector fail on run; error in action log
Re-authorize immediately — admin must re-complete OAuth flow
Goals fail; admin receives workspace alert
Re-authorize; check if provider policy change caused revocation
Goals may fail on specific actions; scope mismatch in log
Re-authorize with updated scope; review all goals using this connector

Each workspace connector is authorized by a specific Cowork admin using their personal OAuth credentials. If that admin leaves the organization and their identity provider account is deactivated, the connector's token is immediately revoked — taking down every goal that uses it.

The mitigation is to authorize workspace connectors using a shared service account rather than a personal account wherever the connector provider supports it. For Google connectors, this means a shared Google account (e.g., cowork-service@acme.com); for Slack, a dedicated Slack app OAuth, not a personal token.
authorize connectors with accounts that are not tied to any individual's employment status
record which service account was used for each connector in a secure internal doc, so any admin can re-authorize if needed
check the workspace connector list each week for warning badges; set up connector health alerts to notify admins of failures
Auditing Connector Scope After Team Changes

When a team reorganizes — a product area merges, a team splits, a project concludes — the connector scopes that were correct six months ago may now be over-broad or mis-aligned. Schedule a connector scope review whenever there is a significant organizational change:
List all workspace connectors and the scope configured for each
List all active goals and the connectors each one uses
For each connector, identify whether any currently-active goal still needs its full scope
Narrow the scope of connectors whose breadth is no longer justified by active goals
Revoke connectors that no active goal uses — if a goal needs the connector later, re-authorize with the correct scope at that time

As the organization grows, the number of workspaces grows too. An org admin overseeing five workspaces faces a governance challenge: ensuring that each workspace's goals are operating safely, that connector authorizations remain appropriate, and that no workspace has become a shadow IT operation running goals nobody understands.
Establish a quarterly review cycle across all workspaces. The org admin exports the goal inventory from each workspace and reviews it against three criteria:
every shared goal has a named current owner who is still with the organization and understands the goal
shared goals that have not run in 90 days are candidates for archiving unless there is a documented reason for dormancy
goals whose connector scope is broader than their documented objective are flagged for scope reduction

In large organizations, the same workflow is often automated independently in two or three workspaces. A weekly sales pipeline summary goal may exist in both the Sales Ops workspace and the Executive workspace, each maintaining its own instruction set, connector scope, and approval configuration. This duplication is wasteful and creates consistency risk — the two goals may produce different summaries from the same data.

When the org admin discovers duplicate goals across workspaces, the correct resolution is to designate one workspace as the authoritative source for that workflow, move the goal there, and give the other workspace's stakeholders Viewer access to the goal's output project. This concentrates maintenance in one place while preserving access for all consumers.

Workspaces, like goals, have a lifecycle. Create them when a team or project needs an isolated operating environment; archive them when the team disbands or the project concludes. An archived workspace's goals stop running and its connector authorizations are revoked, but the goal history and action logs remain accessible to org admins for 12 months after archiving.

Archive rather than delete. A deleted workspace loses its audit trail permanently. If there is any chance an archived workspace's action log will be needed for compliance or dispute resolution, archive it and let the 12-month retention period run before deletion.
Cowork's team and enterprise features — workspaces, roles, shared projects, SSO, and audit logging — are designed to make agentic AI scalable and governable in organizational settings.
the isolation boundary between teams; each workspace has independent connectors, users, and policies
per-workspace; Admin governs connectors and policy, Member creates and runs goals, Viewer and Guest provide read-only access for stakeholders
the team's goal library with shared memory; changes to project memory propagate to all goals in the project
enterprise-grade authentication and automatic provisioning/deprovisioning via existing IdP infrastructure
immutable event log across all workspaces, exportable to SIEM and compliance systems
sandbox-first, minimum scope, shared projects, then incremental automation as goals prove reliable

The governance gap in most team deployments is not the technology — it is the absence of a named owner for each shared goal, a documented rationale for each authorized connector, and a periodic review of the goal library. These practices, not the platform features themselves, determine whether team Cowork use scales successfully.
